title: BaaS authority map shipped in browser artifact
message: A browser artifact exposes Firebase/Supabase config together with sensitive collections or authorization fields.
help: Client BaaS config is often public, but shipped collection names plus owner, role, tenant, or admin fields give attackers a precise authorization map. Verify rules/RLS enforce every boundary server-side.

title: Secret shipped in browser artifact
message: A browser-delivered artifact contains a secret-looking credential value.
help: Remove the secret from client bundles/static assets, rotate it, and route privileged service calls through server-only code.

title: Array lookup inside a loop
message: This scales poorly because `array.includes()` inside a loop scans the whole list every time. Use a Set for constant-time lookups.
help: Use a `Set` or `Map` when you check for the same items over and over. `Array.includes`/`find` scans the whole list each time

title: Derived value copied into state
message: Storing "mode" in state when you can derive it from other values costs an extra render.
help: Work out the value while rendering (or with useMemo if it's expensive) instead of copying it into useState through a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#updating-state-based-on-props-or-state

title: Effect re-subscribes on a changing callback
message: Your effect re-subscribes whenever "onClose" changes, even though it's only used inside `addEventListener`.
help: Wrap the callback with `useEffectEvent(callback)` (React 19+) and call it inside the sub-handler. An Effect Event always sees the latest props and state but isn't a dependency, so the effect won't re-subscribe every time the parent redraws. See https://react.dev/reference/react/useEffectEvent

title: Custom modal instead of dialog
message: Keyboard users can tab out of this `role="dialog"` modal because it has no built-in focus trapping, so use the native `<dialog>`, which gives you focus trapping, `Escape` to close, and the backdrop for free.
help: Replace the wrapper with `<dialog>` and open it with `dialog.showModal()`. For the trigger, prefer `<button commandfor="id" command="show-modal">` (Chrome 135+), or a `useRef` with `dialogRef.current?.showModal()`.

title: Empty default prop breaks memo
message: This keeps redrawing children that compare props because default prop value [] makes a brand new array every render, so move it to a constant at the top of the file
help: Move it to the top of the file: `const EMPTY_ITEMS: Item[] = []`, then use that as the default value

title: Empty default prop breaks memo
message: This keeps redrawing children that compare props because default prop value [] makes a brand new array every render, so move it to a constant at the top of the file
help: Move it to the top of the file: `const EMPTY_ITEMS: Item[] = []`, then use that as the default value

title: Empty default prop breaks memo
message: This keeps redrawing children that compare props because default prop value [] makes a brand new array every render, so move it to a constant at the top of the file
help: Move it to the top of the file: `const EMPTY_ITEMS: Item[] = []`, then use that as the default value

title: Chained array iterations
message: This loops over your list twice because .filter().map() makes two passes, so do it in one pass with .reduce() or a for...of loop
help: Combine `.map().filter()` style chains into one pass with `.reduce()` or a `for...of` loop, so you only loop over the list once

title: Custom modal instead of dialog
message: Keyboard users can tab out of this `role="dialog"` modal because it has no built-in focus trapping, so use the native `<dialog>`, which gives you focus trapping, `Escape` to close, and the backdrop for free.
help: Replace the wrapper with `<dialog>` and open it with `dialog.showModal()`. For the trigger, prefer `<button commandfor="id" command="show-modal">` (Chrome 135+), or a `useRef` with `dialogRef.current?.showModal()`.

title: Role used instead of HTML tag
message: Screen reader users get more reliable semantics from `<dialog>` than `role="dialog"`, so use `<dialog>` instead.
help: Use the matching HTML element when one exists so browsers and assistive tech get native semantics.

title: Derived state stored in an effect
message: Your users briefly see stale state on every prop change.
help: Work out derived values while rendering: `const x = fn(dep)`. To reset a component's state when a prop changes, give it a key prop: `<Component key={prop} />`. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect

title: All state reset on prop change
message: Your users briefly see stale state when a prop changes because this useEffect clears all state.
help: Pass the prop as `key` so React resets the component for you when the prop changes, instead of clearing every state value by hand in a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#resetting-all-state-when-a-prop-changes

title: State synced to a prop inside an effect
message: This effect adjusts state after a prop changes, so users briefly see the stale value.
help: Adjust the state inline during render with a `prev`-prop comparison (`if (prop !== prevProp) { setPrevProp(prop); setX(...); }`), or refactor to remove the duplicated state. Routing the adjustment through a useEffect forces an extra render with a stale UI between the two commits. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#adjusting-some-state-when-a-prop-changes

message: Screen reader users get more reliable semantics from `<address>` than `role="group"`, so use `<address>` instead.

title: Multiple setState calls in one effect
message: 3 setState calls in one useEffect redraw your screen each time they run together.
help: Combine related updates in `useReducer` so one effect does not redraw the screen once per `setState` call.

title: State synced to a prop inside an effect
message: This effect adjusts state after a prop changes, so users briefly see the stale value.
help: Adjust the state inline during render with a `prev`-prop comparison (`if (prop !== prevProp) { setPrevProp(prop); setX(...); }`), or refactor to remove the duplicated state. Routing the adjustment through a useEffect forces an extra render with a stale UI between the two commits. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#adjusting-some-state-when-a-prop-changes

title: Ref initializer runs on every render
message: useRef(new GameEngine()) rebuilds this value on every render & throws it away.
help: Initialize the ref lazily so expensive values are not rebuilt and discarded on every render.

title: State initializer runs on every render
message: useState(getState()) re-runs getState() on every render & throws the result away.
help: Wrap expensive initial state in an arrow function so the initializer does not rerun and get thrown away on every render.

title: Derived value copied into state
message: Storing "times" in state when you can derive it from other values costs an extra render.
help: Work out the value while rendering (or with useMemo if it's expensive) instead of copying it into useState through a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#updating-state-based-on-props-or-state

title: Missing effect dependencies
message: `useEffect` can run with a stale `reset, turn` & show your users old data.

title: Event logic handled in an effect
message: Faking an event handler with a prop plus a useEffect costs an extra render & runs late.
help: Run the side effect in the event handler that triggers it, instead of watching its state from a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#sharing-logic-between-event-handlers

title: Event logic handled in an effect
message: Faking an event handler with state plus a useEffect costs an extra render & runs late.
help: Run the side effect in the event handler that triggers it, instead of watching its state from a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#sharing-logic-between-event-handlers

title: Derived value copied into state
message: Storing "times" in state when you can derive it from other values costs an extra render.
help: Work out the value while rendering (or with useMemo if it's expensive) instead of copying it into useState through a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#updating-state-based-on-props-or-state

title: State updates chained through effects
message: Chaining state updates triggers an extra render each step.
help: Set all the related state together in the event handler that starts it, instead of having one useEffect react to a state change and set more state. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#chains-of-computations

title: Derived value copied into state
message: Storing "lastTurn" in state when you can derive it from other values costs an extra render.
help: Work out the value while rendering (or with useMemo if it's expensive) instead of copying it into useState through a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#updating-state-based-on-props-or-state

title: Multiple setState calls in one effect
message: 5 setState calls in one useEffect redraw your screen each time they run together.
help: Combine related updates in `useReducer` so one effect does not redraw the screen once per `setState` call.

title: State synced to a prop inside an effect
message: This effect adjusts state after a prop changes, so users briefly see the stale value.
help: Adjust the state inline during render with a `prev`-prop comparison (`if (prop !== prevProp) { setPrevProp(prop); setX(...); }`), or refactor to remove the duplicated state. Routing the adjustment through a useEffect forces an extra render with a stale UI between the two commits. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#adjusting-some-state-when-a-prop-changes

title: Missing effect dependencies
message: `useEffect` can run with a stale `state.history.length, state, options` & show your users old data.

title: Many related useState calls
message: 6 useState calls in "ChallengeFriend" can each trigger a separate render.
help: Group related state in `useReducer` so one logical update does not fan out into separate renders.

title: Mutable value in effect dependencies
message: Values like "location.*" can change without re-rendering the component, so this dependency will not make the effect run again.
help: Read mutable values like `location.pathname` or `ref.current` inside the effect body, or subscribe with `useSyncExternalStore`. Changing them doesn't redraw the screen, so listing them in deps won't make the effect run again.

title: Missing effect dependencies
message: `useMemo` re-runs whenever `version` changes even though it never uses it.

title: Unversioned localStorage key
message: sessionStorage.setItem("resume-game", JSON.stringify(...)) has no version, so changing the data shape later crashes your users' saved sessions. Add one to the key (e.g. "resume-game:v1").
help: Put a version in the storage key (e.g. "myKey:v1"). If you change the data shape later, old saved data can be ignored instead of crashing the app.

title: Many related useState calls
message: 5 useState calls in "PlayFriend" can each trigger a separate render.
help: Group related state in `useReducer` so one logical update does not fan out into separate renders.

title: Derived value copied into state
message: Storing "game" in state when you can derive it from other values costs an extra render.
help: Work out the value while rendering (or with useMemo if it's expensive) instead of copying it into useState through a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#updating-state-based-on-props-or-state

title: Missing effect dependencies
message: `useEffect` can run with a stale `chess.loadState` & show your users old data.

title: await inside a loop
message: This makes the for-loop slow because each await runs one after another, so collect the independent calls & run them together with `await Promise.all(items.map(...))`
help: Collect the items, then use `await Promise.all(items.map(...))` so independent work runs at the same time

title: React 19 API migration can break callers
message: useContext is replaced by `use()` in React 19, which reads context inside ifs & loops too, so switch to `import { use } from 'react'`.
help: Pass `ref` as a normal prop on function components, since `forwardRef` isn't needed in React 19. Replace `useContext(X)` with `use(X)`. Only runs on React 19+ projects.

help: Pass `ref` as a normal prop on function components, since `forwardRef` isn't needed in React 19. Replace `useContext(X)` with `use(X)`. Only runs on React 19+ projects.
